claims.subject: work hardening rate in Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Work hardening rate in polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys shows a sharp maximum for alloys containing 26-30% Al.
claims.evidence_text: A sharp maximum in work hardening is seen for alloys containing 26–30% Al.

claims.subject: work hardening rate variation with Al content
claims.claim: Work hardening rate is low for disordered Fe-Al with less than 20% Al, rises to a sharp peak at 28-29% Al, and falls to a moderate value above about 35% Al.
claims.evidence_text: The work hardening rate is seen to be low for disordered Fe–Al with less than 20% Al, rises to a sharp peak at 28–29% Al, and falls to a moderate value above about 35% Al.

claims.subject: yield stress in Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Yield stress in polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys shows a steadily increasing trend with Al content up to 25% Al, a rapid fall at 25-28% Al, and little change or a slight increase over the 30-40% Al range.
claims.evidence_text: The data on yield stress (taken from Refs. [2,5,8], and present study) show a steadily increasing yield stress with Al content up to 25% Al, a rapid fall at 25–28% Al, and little change or a slight increase over the 30–40% Al range.

claims.subject: ductility in Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Ductility in polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys shows an essentially inverse relationship to yield stress, with a rapid fall to 25% Al, a slight maximum at 28% Al, and a steady slow fall thereafter.
claims.evidence_text: Ductility (data taken from Ref. [2]) shows an essentially inverse relationship to the yield stress, with a rapid fall in ductility to 25% Al, a slight maximum at 28% Al, and a steady, slow fall thereafter.

claims.subject: dislocation structure changes near work hardening peak
claims.claim: The most important change in dislocation structures near the work hardening peak is the formation of superdislocation dipoles instead of single dislocation dipoles, predominance of straight screw dislocations, and formation of sessile dislocation segments by reactions of mobile dislocations.
claims.evidence_text: Examination of dislocation structures with increasing Al content over the region of the maximum shows that the most important change, apart from the formation of superdislocation dipoles instead of single dislocation dipoles, is the predominance of straight screw dislocations and the formation of sessile dislocation segments by reactions of mobile dislocations.

claims.subject: superdislocation dipole strengthening for high work hardening
claims.claim: The high work hardening rate of the ordered state has been interpreted using a dipole hardening mechanism, whereby the strengthening effect of the superdislocation dipole is greater than that of the single dislocation dipole.
claims.evidence_text: The high work hardening rate of the ordered state has been interpreted [6,9,10] using a dipole hardening mechanism [14,15], whereby the strengthening effect of the superdislocation dipole is greater than that of the single dislocation dipole.

claims.subject: alternative APB tube theory for high work hardening
claims.claim: An alternative theory based on tubes of anti-phase boundaries (APB) being dragged behind jogs formed on superdislocations has also been suggested for the high work hardening rate of the ordered state.
claims.evidence_text: An alternative theory based on tubes of anti-phase boundaries (APB) being dragged behind jogs formed on superdislocations [16–18] has also been suggested.

claims.subject: dislocation type changes with strain in given alloy
claims.claim: Changes of work hardening rate with increasing strain of a given alloy have been interpreted based on the dipole forming model as due to changes in the type of dislocation present: four (1/2)<111> partials in DO3 order, two (1/2)<111> partials in B2 order, and single (1/2)<111> dislocations in disordered material.
claims.evidence_text: based on the dipole forming model, as due to changes in the type of dislocation present—four partial dislocations each of Burgers vector (1/2)<111> constituting the perfect superdislocation when DO3 order is present; two partial dislocations also of Burgers vector (1/2)<111> constituting the perfect superdislocation when B2 order is present; single (1/2)<111> dislocations present in disordered or short range ordered material.

claims.subject: peak in work hardening rate vs elastic anisotropy
claims.claim: The peak in work hardening rate coincides with increased elastic anisotropy, but this is believed to reflect the importance of core/Peierls effects rather than to be a direct cause of high work hardening.
claims.evidence_text: The peak in work hardening rate coincides with increased elastic anisotropy, but this is believed to reflect the importance of core/Peierls effects rather than to be a direct cause of high work hardening.

claims.subject: APB energies in ordered Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: APB energies in B2 and DO3 ordered Fe-Al alloys vary with Al content, with high APB energies keeping partial dislocations close together.
claims.evidence_text: Data on APB energies are given in Fig. 3, taken from studies of Ray–Crawford–Cockayne [19–21]. High APB energies will tend to keep partial dislocations close together.

claims.subject: elastic anisotropy in Fe3Al alloys
claims.claim: Fe3Al alloys have an extremely high degree of elastic anisotropy, reflected in the Zener anisotropy factor A = 2C44/(C11-C12), due to an increase in C44 and a fall in (C11-C12)/2.
claims.evidence_text: Fe3Al alloys are seen to have an extremely high degree of elastic anisotropy, as reflected in the Zener anisotropy factor, A = 2C44/(C11-C12) ... due to both an increase in the value of the C44 parameter and a fall in (C11-C12)/2.

context_update.summary: This front matter chunk (Abstract and Introduction) of the paper 'Work hardening in Fe–Al alloys' introduces the study of work hardening in polycrystalline Fe–Al alloys with Al content up to 40 at.%. It states the key finding of a sharp maximum in work hardening rate near 26-30 at.% Al, and attributes this to a transition from single to superdislocation dipoles, predominance of straight screw dislocations, and sessile segment formation. It reviews literature data on yield stress, ductility, and work hardening rate as functions of Al content (Figs. 1-2). Two main mechanisms are cited for high work hardening in ordered states: dipole hardening and APB tube dragging. Dislocation types are linked to ordering state (DO3, B2, disordered). APB energies (Fig. 3) and elastic anisotropy (Fig. 4) are presented as influential factors. The chunk also clarifies that specimens are thermally-relaxed as-cast, coarse-grained, with no solute/precipitate effects, and that ordering state (slowly cooled) is considered. The study aims to interpret work hardening variation via dislocation structures.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Fe-Al alloys were prepared by induction melting in an alumina crucible under inert gas and drop cast into a 25 mm diameter cylindrical copper mould to produce a grain size of the order of 0.25 mm.
claims.evidence_text: A series of Fe–Al base alloys was prepared using standard induction melting in an alumina crucible under inert gas and drop casting into a 25 mm diameter cylindrical copper mould to produce samples with grain size of the order of 0.25 mm.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Binary alloys with Al contents of 15, 20, 25, 28, 30 and 35% Al were prepared, as well as alloys with 20 and 30% Al containing 0.1% B.
claims.evidence_text: Binary alloys with Al contents of 15, 20, 25, 28, 30 and 35% Al were prepared, as well as some alloys with 20 and 30% Al containing an addition of 0.1% B (atomic percent throughout).

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Cooling after solidification was sufficiently slow to allow a high degree of B2 order for Al-rich alloys and a good degree of DO3 order for 25-30% Al alloys, as indicated by strong superlattice spots and coarse DO3 domains in TEM.
claims.evidence_text: Cooling after solidification was sufficiently slow to allow a high degree of B2 order for alloys rich in Al, and a good degree of DO3 order for alloys containing 25–30% Al, as indicated by strong superlattice spots and coarse DO3 domains observed during subsequent transmission electron microscopy.

claims.subject: compression samples
claims.claim: Compression samples were cut from ingots by spark machining as 3 mm diameter or 5 mm x 5 mm square cross-section samples with height twice the diameter or width, polished on fine abrasive paper, and tested at room temperature to 2-3% strain at a strain rate of 3e-4 s-1.
claims.evidence_text: Compression samples for mechanical testing were cut from the ingots by spark machining, either as 3 mm diameter or as 5 mm 5 mm square cross-section samples, of height twice the diameter or width. Flat surfaces were polished on fine (1500 grade) abrasive paper before testing, at room temperature, to a strain of 2–3% at a nominal strain rate of 3e-4 s-1.

claims.subject: compression tests
claims.claim: Compression tests showed a smooth onset of plasticity with no discrete yield points and slightly curved stress-strain behavior with no linear regions.
claims.evidence_text: Compression tests showed a smooth onset of plasticity, with no discrete yield points observed, and the stress–strain behaviour was always slightly curved, with no sign of the linear stress–strain regions sometimes observed.

claims.subject: TEM sample preparation
claims.claim: Thin foils for TEM were prepared from deformed samples using twin-jet electropolishing with 25% nitric acid in methanol at about 20 V and -30 degrees C.
claims.evidence_text: thin foils prepared for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a twin-jet electropolishing unit with a 25% nitric acid in methanol mixture at about 20 V and -30 degrees C.

claims.subject: TEM examination
claims.claim: Samples were examined using a JEOL 2010 microscope with dislocations imaged in Bright Field and Weak Beam modes, and planar faults in dark field modes.
claims.evidence_text: Samples were examined using a JEOL 2010 microscope with dislocations imaged in Bright Field and Weak Beam modes, and planar (shear or thermal) faults observed in various dark field modes.

claims.subject: yield stress and work hardening rate
claims.claim: Data on yield stress and work hardening rate from present tests are consistent with prior literature and show a work hardening peak maximum at 29% Al over the 1-1.5% strain range, with the maximum anywhere in 26-29% Al.
claims.evidence_text: Data on yield stress and work hardening rate obtained in the present tests are included with the prior literature data in Figs. 1 and 2, and seem to be consistent with these prior data. the work hardening peak maximum at 29% Al, for work hardening measured over the 1–1.5% strain range, was similar to the peak observed using other strain ranges, but the maximum was found anywhere in the 26–29% Al range.

claims.subject: B addition to Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Addition of B to Fe-Al alloys led to only a slight increase in flow stress relative to B-free samples with little consistent change in work hardening rate.
values: little consistent change in work hardening rate
claims.evidence_text: Addition of B to the alloys led to only a slight increase in flow stress relative to the B-free samples, with little consistent change either in the work hardening rate, as seen in Fig. 2.

claims.subject: disordered Fe-Al alloys (15 and 20% Al)
claims.claim: Disordered Fe-Al materials (15 and 20% Al) deform by movement of single dislocations of Burgers vector (1/2)<111>, which are smoothly curved with some preference for screw orientations and show weak clustering as incipient cell structures.
claims.evidence_text: Disordered materials, or those containing some level of short range order, are deformed by the movement of single dislocations of Burgers vector (1/2) 1 1 1, as illustrated in Figs. 5–7, showing dislocations in materials containing 15 and 20% Al. The single dislocations were generally smoothly curved, Fig. 6, showing some preference for orientations as screw dislocations, Fig. 7, and in thicker regions of the foils it was seen that there was some tendency for a weak clustering of dislocations as an incipient cell structure, Fig. 5.

claims.subject: disordered Fe-Al alloys (15 and 20% Al)
claims.claim: Many dislocation segments in clustered regions and bowed dislocations in Fe-20% Al are dipoles, confirmed by relative shift of images on changing imaging vector sign.
claims.evidence_text: Many of the dislocation segments in the clustered regions, Fig. 5, were seen to be dipoles, as were many of the bowed dislocations in Fig. 6. The dipole nature of these segments, and occasional small loops, is confirmed by the relative shift of the images on changing from a positive to a negative imaging vector (indicated as d in Fig. 6).

claims.subject: Fe-20% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-20% Al, straight screw dislocations are seen together with narrow edge dipoles and many small dipole loops produced by trailing from gliding screw dislocations.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 7 shows another region of thin foil in the Fe–20% Al material where some straight screw dislocations are seen, together with some narrow edge dipoles and many small dipole loops. These loops and dipoles are seen to be produced by trailing from the gliding screw dislocations.

claims.subject: Fe-25% Al with DO3 order
claims.claim: In Fe-25% Al with DO3 order, dislocations remained straight in near-screw orientations with edge dipole segments and loosely coupled screw dipole configurations; APB energies for APBI and APBII faults were insufficiently high to retain closely coupled superdislocations.
claims.evidence_text: With the appearance of long range order in the Fe–25% Al alloy showing DO3 order, dislocations remained more straight as well as in near-screw orientations and again showed edge dipole segments as well as loosely coupled screw dipole configurations, Figs. 8 and 9. The APB energies for both APBI and APBII faults were insufficiently high to retain dislocations together as closely coupled superdislocations, as seen in Fig. 8.

claims.subject: Fe-25% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-25% Al, loosely coupled dislocation pairs are superdislocation partials (p) or loose dipole pairs (d), with segments remaining more straight and close to screw orientation compared to Fe-20% Al.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 9 shows a large number of near-screw dislocations in the deformed Fe–25% Al alloy, where many loose pairs of dislocations can be distinguished. Examination using +g and g imaging conditions shows that many of the pairs are loose superdislocation partials (marked p), while others are loose dipole pairs (marked d), similar to those seen in Fe–20% Al material, Fig. 6, except that segments tend to remain more straight and close to the screw orientation.

claims.subject: Fe-28% Al at work hardening peak
claims.claim: In Fe-28% Al at the work hardening peak, dislocations are invariably superdislocations composed of two (1/2)<111> partials (perfect for B2, imperfect for DO3), trailing APBII faults but not APBI faults.
claims.evidence_text: dislocations are now invariably superdislocations composed of two (1/2) 1 1 1 partials—that is they constitute a perfect superdislocation for the B2 lattice, but an imperfect superdislocation for the DO3 lattice, and hence trail APBII faults as they glide. These superdislocations do not trail any APBI faults.

claims.subject: Fe-28% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-28% Al, dislocations are generally straight and mostly in near-screw orientations along {110} glide planes; many are arranged in dipole configurations or are non-dipole pairs with identical Burgers vectors.
claims.evidence_text: dislocations are generally straight, and are mostly in near-screw orientations. Fig. 10 shows large numbers of rather straight superdislocations of several different Burgers vector often oriented in near-screw directions along their {1 1 0} glide planes. many [dislocations] are arranged in dipole configurations, marked as d in Fig. 11, while other pairs have identical Burgers vectors, marked as p in Fig. 11.

claims.subject: Fe-28% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-28% Al, reaction of two superdislocations with different <111> Burgers vectors in different {110} glide planes produces a pair of edge dislocations with <100> Burgers vector in sessile configuration on a cube plane, acting as locks.
claims.evidence_text: a reaction of two superdislocations with different 1 1 1 Burgers vectors and lying in different {1 1 0} glide planes producing a pair of edge dislocations with 1 0 0 Burgers vector sitting in a sessile configuration on a cube plane. such configurations constitute locks that prevent further room temperature superdislocation motion.

claims.subject: Fe-30% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-30% Al, deformation occurs by movement of superdislocations composed of two (1/2)<111> partials, with straight near-screw dislocations and edge dislocation dipoles and loops.
claims.evidence_text: Deformation occurs by the movement of the same superdislocations composed of 2 (1/2) 1 1 1 partial dislocations, with many straight near-screw dislocations seen, as well as many edge dislocation dipoles and loops, illustrated in Fig. 14.

claims.subject: Fe-30% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-30% Al, APBI fault energy is sufficiently high that the separation of partials is small, and parallel image pairs correspond to short or long dipole dislocation pairs.
claims.evidence_text: The APBI fault energy is now sufficiently high that the separation of the partials constituting the superdislocation is small, and the parallel image pairs visible in Fig. 14 (right) all correspond to short or long dipole dislocations pairs.

claims.subject: Fe-30% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-30% Al, superdislocations are often straight in screw orientations with reactions leading to stepped structures; dislocation pairs are from reactions, not glissile superdislocation partials.
claims.evidence_text: Superdislocations are often rather straight in screw orientations, with reactions between dislocations leading to stepped structures, see Fig. 15. Dislocation pairs observed here, as also in Fig. 16, are the result of dislocation reactions, and do not correspond to the partials of the glissile superdislocations.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-35% Al, dislocations are superdislocations of the B2 lattice with two (1/2)<111> partials lying close together due to higher APBI fault energy; many straight near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments are present.
claims.evidence_text: The dislocations present are superdislocations of the B2 lattice, constituted by two partials of Burgers vector (1/2) 1 1 1 each, with these partials lying close to each other due to the higher APBI fault energy. Fig. 17 shows many straight, near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-35% Al, dipole segments are pulled out of moving screw superdislocations when encountering obstacles.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 17 also shows (at right) several dipole segments being pulled out of the moving screw superdislocations where these encounter some obstacle.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al
claims.claim: In Fe-35% Al, the presence of some DO3 order means superdislocations with partial pairs destroy DO3 order by movement, indicated by weak remnant fringes.
claims.evidence_text: the presence of some degree of DO3 order means that these superdislocations (with a pair of partial dislocations) again destroy some of the DO3 order by their movement, which is indicated by the weak remnant fringes seen in Fig. 19.

claims.subject: Fig. 5
claims.claim: Fig. 5 shows single dislocations arranged in a loose cellular distribution in Fe-15% Al deformed 2-3% with zone axis near [013] and imaging g vector 200.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 5. Single dislocations arranged in a loose cellular distribution in Fe–15% Al deformed 2–3%. Zone axis (stress axis) is close to [0 1 3] and imaging g vector (arrowed) is 2 0 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 6
claims.claim: Fig. 6 shows curled single dislocations in Fe-20% Al with zone axis [011] and imaging vector 011, showing dipole arrangement via opposite sign imaging vectors.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 6. Curled single dislocations in Fe–20% Al. Zone axis (stress axis) is [0 1 1] and imaging vector (arrowed) parallel to 011. Opposite sign imaging vectors were used for the two photographs, and relative displacement of images indicates the dipole arrangement of many dislocation segments and loops.

claims.subject: Fig. 7
claims.claim: Fig. 7 shows straight screw dislocations in Fe-20% Al with zone axis near [001] and imaging g vector 110, showing edge dipole segments and loops.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 7. Straight, screw dislocations, sometimes loosely paired, and edge dipole segments and loops in Fe–20% Al. Zone axis near [0 0 1], imaging g vector 1 1 0 (arrowed).

claims.subject: Fig. 8
claims.claim: Fig. 8 shows APB faults and dislocations in deformed Fe-25% Al with zone axis near [001] and imaging g vector 100, showing APBI faults from (1/2)111 dislocation movement.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 8. Dark field (left) and weak beam (right) micrographs showing APB faults and dislocations in deformed Fe–25% Al. For both images the zone axis is near [0 0 1] and imaging g vector is parallel to 1 0 0. It also shows APBI faults (marked s) produced by movement of (1/2) 1 1 1 dislocations (B2 convention).

claims.subject: Fig. 9
claims.claim: Fig. 9 shows dislocation pairs (p) and dipoles (d) in Fe-25% Al after deformation with zone axis near [001] and 110 imaging vector.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 9. Bright field transmission electron micrograph showing dislocation pairs (p) and dipoles (d) in Fe–25% Al after deformation. Zone axis is near [0 0 1] and a 1 1 0 imaging vector (arrowed) is used for contrast.

claims.subject: Fig. 10
claims.claim: Fig. 10 shows many superdislocations, generally straight and in near-screw orientations, in deformed Fe-28% Al with zone axis near [001] and imaging vector 200.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 10. Bright field micrograph showing many superdislocations, generally straight and in near-screw orientations, in deformed Fe–28% Al. Zone axis is near [0 0 1] and imaging vector (arrowed) is 2 0 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 11
claims.claim: Fig. 11 shows dipole pairs (d) and non-dipole dislocation pairs (p) in deformed Fe-28% Al imaged with +/- 200 g vectors.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 11. Pair of bright field micrographs in +g and g imaging conditions showing dipole pairs (marked d) and non-dipole dislocation pairs (marked p) in deformed Fe–28% Al. Zone axis is near [0 0 1] and imaging g vector (arrowed) are +/- 2 0 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 12
claims.claim: Fig. 12 shows straight and parallel reaction dislocations (r) in deformed Fe-28% Al imaged with +/- g of type 110.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 12. Pair of bright field micrographs in +g and g imaging conditions showing straight and parallel reaction dislocations (marked r) in deformed Fe–28% Al. Zone axis near [0 0 1] and imaging vector (marked) of type 1 1 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 13
claims.claim: Fig. 13 is a weak beam micrograph showing superdislocation reactions (r) in deformed Fe-28% Al with zone axis near [001] and imaging g vector 200.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 13. Weak beam micrograph showing superdislocation reactions (marked r) in deformed Fe–28% Al. Insets show reaction dislocation in more detail. Zone axis near [0 0 1]; imaging g vector (arrowed) is 2 0 0. Imaging condition near g:3–4g.

claims.subject: Fig. 14
claims.claim: Fig. 14 shows typical dislocation structures in deformed Fe-30% Al with zone axis near [111] and imaging vector 1-10, showing dipole segments and loops.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 14. Low magnification (left) and higher magnification (right) bright field micrographs showing typical dislocation structures in deformed Fe–30% Al. Paired dislocations evident in the image at right (marked d) are generally dipole segments and loops. Zone axis near [1 1 1], imaging vector (arrowed) is 1 1 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 15
claims.claim: Fig. 15 shows straight dislocation segments in deformed Fe-30% Al with zone axis near [001] and imaging vector 110.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 15. Bright field micrograph showing straight dislocation segments in deformed Fe–30% Al. Zone axis is near [0 0 1] and imaging vector (arrowed) is 1 1 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 16
claims.claim: Fig. 16 shows dislocation segments and background fringes in deformed Fe-30% Al with zone axis near [001] and imaging vector 110.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 16. Bright field micrograph showing dislocation segments and background fringes in deformed Fe–30% Al. Zone axis is near [0 0 1] and imaging vector (arrowed) is 1 1 0.

claims.subject: Fig. 17
claims.claim: Fig. 17 shows straight near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments in deformed Fe-35% Al.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 17 shows many straight, near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments, seen as widely spaced dislocations.

claims.subject: Fig. 18
claims.claim: Fig. 18 shows a large number of dipole segments in deformed Fe-35% Al imaged under +/- g conditions.
claims.evidence_text: The large number of dipole segments formed are clearly seen in the pair of images shown in Fig. 18 imaged under +/- g conditions.

claims.subject: Fig. 19
claims.claim: Fig. 19 shows weak remnant fringes indicating DO3 order destruction in deformed Fe-35% Al.
claims.evidence_text: the weak remnant fringes seen in Fig. 19.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the Experimental section and the beginning of Results (mechanical properties and dislocation structures). It details sample preparation: induction melting and drop casting into a 25 mm copper mould producing coarse grains (~0.25 mm). Binary alloys with 15, 20, 25, 28, 30, 35% Al were made, plus B-doped versions at 20 and 30% Al. Slow cooling gave B2 order in Al-rich alloys and DO3 order in 25-30% Al. Compression samples were spark-cut to 3 mm diameter or 5x5 mm square, polished, and tested at room temperature to 2-3% strain at 3e-4 s-1. Tests showed smooth plasticity with no yield points. TEM sample preparation used twin-jet electropolishing. Mechanical results confirm prior data with work hardening peak at 29% Al (range 26-29%). B addition had little effect. Dislocation structures vary with Al content: disordered alloys (15-20% Al) show single (1/2)<111> dislocations, curved with incipient cell structure and dipole formation. At 25% Al (DO3 order), loosely coupled superdislocations with APB faults appear. At 28% Al (peak hardening), superdislocations are straight near-screw, and reactions produce sessile <100> edge locks. At 30% and 35% Al, similar superdislocation structures with higher APB energy lead to closer partials and more dipole segments. APBII fringe contrast is observed in some alloys. Figures 5-19 document these structures.
processing_route: induction melting in alumina crucible under inert gas
drop casting into 25 mm diameter copper mould
room temperature compression to 2-3% strain at 3e-4 s-1
twin-jet electropolishing for TEM (25% nitric acid in methanol at -30 degrees C and 20 V)
microstructure: single dislocations with Burgers vector (1/2)<111> in disordered alloys
smoothly curved dislocations with screw preference (15-20% Al)
dipoles and small loops from trailing screw dislocations (20% Al)
loosely coupled superdislocations and APB faults (25-30% Al)
sessile <100> edge dislocations from reactions (28% Al peak)
edge dipole segments and loops (all alloys)
properties: work hardening rate peak at 26-29% Al
slight flow stress increase with B addition
mechanisms: single dislocation glide in disordered alloys
superdislocation glide with APB fault trailing
dislocation reaction locks (sessile <100> edges) blocking motion
figures_or_tables: Fig. 7 (Fe-20% Al, straight screw dislocations, edge dipoles)
Fig. 8 (Fe-25% Al, APB faults in dark field and weak beam)
Fig. 9 (Fe-25% Al, dislocation pairs and dipoles)
Fig. 11 (Fe-28% Al, dipole and non-dipole pairs)
Fig. 13 (Fe-28% Al, superdislocation reactions, weak beam)
Fig. 14 (Fe-30% Al, dislocation structures and dipoles)
Fig. 16 (Fe-30% Al, segments and APBII fringes)

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al alloy
claims.claim: Fe-35% Al alloy deforms by superdislocations of the B2 lattice, each consisting of two partials of Burgers vector (1/2)<111>, with partials lying close to each other due to higher APBI fault energy.
claims.evidence_text: The dislocations present are superdislocations of the B2 lattice, constituted by two partials of Burgers vector (1/2) 1 1 1 each, with these partials lying close to each other due to the higher APBI fault energy.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al alloy
claims.claim: Deformed Fe-35% Al shows many straight near-screw superdislocations, edge dipole segments, and dipole segments pulled out of moving screw superdislocations when encountering obstacles.
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 17 shows many straight, near-screw superdislocations and edge dipole segments, seen as widely spaced dislocations. Fig. 17 also shows (at right) several dipole segments being pulled out of the moving screw superdislocations where these encounter some obstacle.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al alloy
claims.claim: In Fe-35% Al, presence of some degree of DO3 order causes superdislocations to destroy DO3 order during movement, indicated by weak remnant fringes in Fig. 19.
claims.evidence_text: As for the alloys with 28–30% Al, the presence of some degree of DO3 order means that these superdislocations (with a pair of partial dislocations) again destroy some of the DO3 order by their movement, which is indicated by the weak remnant fringes seen in Fig. 19.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Flow strength increases with Al content to a maximum at 25 at.% Al, attributed to solution hardening and short range order hardening on single dislocations before superdislocations take over.
claims.evidence_text: Flow strength increases with Al content to a maximum at 25%, which has been analysed previously as due to solution hardening and short range order hardening on the single dislocations present [8], until superdislocations take over deformation at higher Al content [6,9,10].

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Ductility drops as Al content increases mostly due to material hardening, with a small ductility peak at about 28 at.% Al not discussed previously.
claims.evidence_text: The ductility drop as Al content increases is mostly due to material hardening, although environmental embrittlement may also play a role. The small ductility peak at about 28% Al has not been discussed previously.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Work hardening is higher for ordered alloys, especially high for compositions near 28 at.% Al.
claims.evidence_text: The main subject of the present work is the work hardening evolution, Fig. 2, which is seen to be higher for the ordered alloys, but especially high for compositions near 28% Al.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Formation of superdislocation dipoles instead of single dislocation dipoles is expected to produce greater hardening for ordered alloys, but no APB tubes were observed to support that model.
claims.evidence_text: The formation of superdislocation dipoles instead of single dislocation dipoles is expected to produce greater hardening for the ordered alloys, as is the formation of APB tubes behind jogs on the superdislocations. The present study has seen large numbers of dipoles after deformation, both on single dislocations (at 15–20% Al) and on superdislocations (at 28–35% Al). This can be seen as a reason for the high work hardening rate of the ordered state. No APB tubes have been observed, so there is little support here for this model of work hardening.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: A peak in work hardening at about 28 at.% Al could possibly be explained by four-partial superdislocations in DO3 order range, but such dislocations were never observed and for 25 at.% Al alloy APBI energy is too low for such dissociation.
claims.evidence_text: A peak could possibly be explained if superdislocations composed of four-partial dislocations existed over a certain range of Al content, especially near 25–28% Al, where DO3 order exists and the corresponding APBI and APBII energies are high. However, such four-partial superdislocations have never been observed here, and indeed for the 25% Al alloy the APBI energy is so low that dislocations appear completely dissociated as single dislocations, trailing the corresponding APB fault.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Dipole formation in disordered Fe-Al alloys is less prevalent because dislocations are curly and mixed edge/screw, while ordered alloys with a predominance of screw dislocations show greater probability of dipole formation.
claims.evidence_text: The disordered alloys with their curly dislocations of mixed edge and screw dislocations will show less prevalence to dipole formation. (There are thus two reasons why the disordered materials will show lower work hardening rate: fewer dipoles, and single dislocation dipoles.)

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: In ordered alloys, work hardening is higher because dipoles are super-dipoles formed on superdislocations, while in disordered alloys low hardening is due to free bowing of dislocations to all configurations (many edge and screw dislocations) and fewer dipoles.
claims.evidence_text: Taking as basic model to explain work hardening the formation of dipoles behind moving screw dislocations, it is clear that work hardening in the ordered alloys will be higher than in the disordered alloys because the dipoles will be super-dipoles formed on superdislocations. The alloy with 25% Al, albeit ordered, will maintain a relatively low work hardening rate since the APB energies are not high enough to ensure that dislocations remain coupled as superdislocations.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: High elastic anisotropy (peak at 25-30 at.% Al) is not sufficient alone to explain the work hardening peak, as the relative energy increase from screw to edge dislocation is only 5-10% less for less anisotropic alloys (30-34 at.% Al).
claims.evidence_text: From Kotter et al. [23] and Yoo et al. [25], the relative energy increase for Fe–25% Al on changing a screw dislocation into an edge is of the order of 1.8–2.7 times, but is only about 5–10% less for the less anisotropic alloys with 30–34% Al. This small difference in energy change as a screw dislocation becomes an edge hardly seems sufficient to change significantly the fraction of screw dislocations present, and thus cause such a large peak in the work hardening rate.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Peierls forces, reflecting changes in interatomic forces, are suggested to be stronger for compositions near 28 at.% Al, ensuring dislocations are strongly oriented along screw directions and increasing dipole pinning.
claims.evidence_text: Accordingly the high elastic anisotropy (much greater at 25–30% Al) is reflected in higher Peierls forces for these same compositions, especially near 28% Al. Indeed, studies of slip plane selection by screw dislocations as in-plane and out-of-plane applied stresses change confirm the importance of core structure and Peierls effects for alloys near the Fe Al composition (compared with Fe) [33]. These Peierls forces ensure that dislocations here are most strongly oriented along screw directions and the tendency to form dipole pinning points is the strongest.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: Reaction of <111> dislocations to produce straight edge <100> dislocations provides additional pinning obstacles in ordered alloys near 28-30 at.% Al; these <100> dislocations have extremely high Peierls forces at room temperature and are strong barriers to glide of <111> superdislocations.
claims.evidence_text: An additional pinning obstacle observed for all ordered alloys, but especially for those near 28–30% Al, is that produced by reaction of <111> dislocations to produce straight edge <100> dislocations. Peierls forces are known to be extremely high on such dislocations at room temperature [33] and hence such dislocations are strong barriers to further glide of <111> superdislocations.

claims.subject: Fe-Al alloys
claims.claim: The small ductility peak near 28 at.% Al (Fig. 1) is associated with the peak in work hardening rate over the composition range 26-30 at.% Al where flow stress is relatively constant.
claims.evidence_text: Around 28% Al, the small ductility maximum can be associated with the peak in work hardening rate over a composition range (26–30% Al) where the flow stress is relatively constant.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al alloy
claims.claim: Fig. 17 shows bright-field micrographs of deformed Fe-35% Al with edge dipole segments pulled out of glissile screw dislocations (zone axis near [110], imaging vector 110).
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 17. Bright field micrographs illustrating typical dislocation structures produced by deforming Fe–35% Al. Many edge dipole segments are visible, some being dragged out of a glissile screw dislocation, at right. For both images, zone axis is near [1 1 0] and imaging vector (arrowed) is 1 1 0.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al alloy
claims.claim: Fig. 18 shows +g and -g imaging of deformed Fe-35% Al revealing many edge dipole segments (zone axis near [113], imaging vector type 110).
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 18. Pair of bright field micrographs in +g and g imaging conditions showing the presence of many edge dipole segments in deformed Fe–35% Al. Zone axis near [1 1 3] and imaging vector (marked) of type 1 1 0.

claims.subject: Fe-35% Al alloy
claims.claim: Fig. 19 shows bright-field micrograph of deformed Fe-35% Al with dislocation segments and background fringes (zone axis near [113], imaging vector 110).
claims.evidence_text: Fig. 19. Bright field micrograph showing dislocation segments and background fringes in deformed Fe–35% Al. Zone axis is near [1 1 3] and imaging vector (arrowed) is 1 1 0.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers final Results (dislocation structures in Fe-35% Al) and the entire Discussion section. For Fe-35% Al, superdislocations of B2 lattice with closely spaced partials, straight near-screw superdislocations, edge dipole segments, and weak remnant DO3 fringes are documented in Figs. 17-19. The Discussion analyzes work hardening origins: flow strength peaks at 25% Al (solution/short-range-order hardening), ductility drops with Al content (with a small peak near 28% Al), and work hardening peaks near 28% Al. High hardening in ordered alloys is linked to superdislocation dipoles; no APB tubes were observed. Four-partial superdislocations were not observed. Elastic anisotropy alone cannot explain the peak; Peierls forces are suggested to make dislocations strongly screw-oriented, enhancing dipole formation. Reaction of <111> dislocations to edge <100> dislocations produces strong barriers, especially in 28-30% Al range. The small ductility peak at ~28% Al is associated with work hardening peak and constant flow stress in 26-30% Al range. The Discussion also references anomalous hardening in L1₂ alloys (Kear-Wilsdorf locks) as analogous. No new sample aliases or processing routes are introduced beyond previous ones.
microstructure: single dislocations in disordered alloys (15-20% Al)
loosely coupled superdislocations with APB faults (25% Al)
sessile <100> edge dislocations from dislocation reactions
properties: ductility (drop with Al, small peak at 28% Al)
mechanisms: dipole formation behind moving screw dislocations
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1 (flow stress and ductility vs Al)

claims.subject: ductility peak in Fe-28% Al alloys
claims.claim: A small ductility peak is observed for alloys with composition near 28% Al, which can be associated with the peak in work hardening rate over a composition range of 26–30% Al where the flow stress is relatively constant.
claims.evidence_text: the small ductility maximum can be associated with the peak in work hardening rate over a composition range (26–30% Al) where the flow stress is relatively constant.

claims.subject: work hardening peak in Fe–Al alloys
claims.claim: Work hardening rate is higher in the ordered than in the disordered state and shows a strong peak near Fe–28% Al, coinciding with a fall in flow stress above 25% Al and a small ductility maximum at 28% Al.
claims.evidence_text: Work hardening rate is seen to be higher in the ordered than in the disordered state and to show a strong peak near Fe–28% Al. This composition coincides with a fall in flow stress above 25% Al and a small ductility maximum at 28% Al.

claims.subject: dislocation structures in disordered and ordered Fe–Al alloys
claims.claim: Dislocation structures after deformation at room temperature show a transition from single, bowed dislocations for disordered materials (15–20% Al) to paired superdislocations with a strong tendency to form straight screw segments for highly ordered materials (28–35% Al).
claims.evidence_text: Dislocation structures after deformation at room temperature show a transition from single, bowed dislocations for disordered materials (15–20% Al) to paired superdislocations with a strong tendency to form straight screw segments for highly ordered materials (28–35% Al).

claims.subject: DO3 ordered Fe–25% Al superdislocations
claims.claim: DO3 ordered Fe–25% Al has sufficiently low APB energies that superdislocations do not form and single dislocations trail shear APB in this material.
claims.evidence_text: DO3 ordered Fe–25% Al has sufficiently low APB energies that superdislocations do not form and single dislocations trail shear APB in this material.

claims.subject: coupled four-partial dislocations in DO3 ordered Fe–Al
claims.claim: For no material was there evidence of the operation of coupled four-partial dislocations which constitute the perfect superdislocation for the DO3 ordered state.
claims.evidence_text: For no material was there evidence of the operation of coupled four-partial dislocations which constitute the perfect superdislocation for the DO3 ordered state.

claims.subject: dipoles in Fe–Al alloys
claims.claim: Large numbers of dipoles were observed in deformed materials of all compositions, being single dislocation dipoles for disordered materials and superdislocation dipoles for ordered materials.
claims.evidence_text: Large numbers of dipoles were observed in deformed materials of all compositions, being single dislocation dipoles for disordered materials and superdislocation dipoles for ordered materials.

claims.subject: factors for high work hardening in ordered Fe–Al alloys
claims.claim: High work hardening is associated with two factors: the presence of superdislocations which produce stronger dipole locks, and the increased tendency of dislocations to line in screw orientations.
claims.evidence_text: High work hardening is associated with two factors: the presence of superdislocations which produce stronger dipole locks, and the increased tendency of dislocations to line in screw orientations.

claims.subject: origin of screw dislocation tendency
claims.claim: It is believed that the tendency for dislocations to lie in screw orientations is due to the strong Peierls forces found for compositions close to 25–30% Al, and not the elastic anisotropy itself.
claims.evidence_text: It is believed that the tendency for dislocations to lie in screw orientations is due to the strong Peierls forces found for compositions close to 25–30% Al, and not the elastic anisotropy itself.

claims.subject: dislocation reactions in Fe–28–30% Al alloys
claims.claim: Dislocation reactions between different 1 1 1 dislocations leading to sessile 1 0 0 edge dislocations are seen for alloys close to the work hardening peak (28–30% Al) which may also contribute to the high work hardening of such alloys.
claims.evidence_text: Dislocation reactions between different 1 1 1 dislocations leading to sessile 1 0 0 edge dislocations are seen for alloys close to the work hardening peak (28–30% Al) which may also contribute to the high work hardening of such alloys.

claims.subject: ductility peak in Fe–28% Al
claims.claim: The small peak in ductility seen for alloys with composition Fe–28% Al can be explained by the high work hardening rate of this material.
claims.evidence_text: The small peak in ductility seen for alloys with composition Fe–28% Al can be explained by the high work hardening rate of this material.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the final part of the Discussion (ductility peak) and the entire Summary section. It reiterates the work hardening peak near Fe-28% Al, which coincides with a fall in flow stress above 25% Al and a small ductility maximum at 28% Al due to the Considere criterion and high work hardening rate. Dislocation structures transition from single bowed dislocations in disordered materials (15-20% Al) to paired superdislocations with straight screw segments in highly ordered materials (28-35% Al). DO3 ordered Fe-25% Al shows no superdislocations due to low APB energies; single dislocations trail shear APB. No coupled four-partial dislocations were observed. Dipoles are present in all compositions, with superdislocation dipoles in ordered materials. High work hardening is linked to stronger dipole locks from superdislocations and screw dislocation tendency due to Peierls forces (not elastic anisotropy) for 25-30% Al. Dislocation reactions producing sessile <100> edges are seen near 28-30% Al, contributing to hardening. The ductility peak at Fe-28% Al is explained by high work hardening rate. No new materials, processing routes, or sample aliases are introduced. References section follows.
microstructure: paired superdislocations with straight screw segments (28-35% Al)
single dislocations trail shear APB (Fe-25% Al)
dipoles: single in disordered, superdislocation in ordered
properties: work hardening rate peak near Fe-28% Al
fall in flow stress above 25% Al
mechanisms: screw orientation from Peierls forces (25-30% Al)
dislocation reactions to sessile <100> edges (28-30% Al)
